meta.title: The Ice That Remembers the Air
meta.theme: how bubbles trapped in glacial ice record ancient atmospheres

format.format_verified: 2026-07: five sentences, TITA, +3 with no negative marking, consistent in released papers 2016 to 2025; re-verify against each new official paper

traps.description: No engineered trap. The outlier is a true, general definitional statement about the scientific method that sits above the specific ice core narrative the four sentences build.

meta.learning_objective: tell a general definitional claim apart from the specific process a paragraph is describing

source.usage_note: Original composition in CAT register for CAT OS. General earth science of ice cores; no specific text adapted. Personal study only.

meta.prompt_version: in-repo authored (Odd One Out milestone); ODD_MAN_OUT_BIBLE frameworks applied
meta.reviewer_notes: Easy tier: a specific expository account of how ice cores preserve air. The outlier is a correct, textbook generalisation about how science works, dropped into the specific process. Teaches general versus specific. Outlier at A. Owner review pending.

sentences.label: A
sentences.text: Science advances by testing predictions against evidence, and a hypothesis survives only as long as the data allow it to.

sentences.label: B
sentences.text: Each winter's snowfall settles on a glacier and is slowly pressed into ice by the weight of the snow that falls above it.

sentences.label: C
sentences.text: As the snow compacts, it traps tiny pockets of the air that surrounded it, sealing a sample of that year's atmosphere inside the ice.

sentences.label: D
sentences.text: Drill a core down through such a glacier, and you recover those pockets layer by layer, oldest at the bottom, most recent near the top.

sentences.label: E
sentences.text: Measuring the gases in each bubble, researchers can read the composition of the air from thousands of years before any instrument existed to record it.

builder.prompt: Build the four sentence account of how ice remembers ancient air, from snowfall to reading the past. The sentence left out is your answer.

explanation.spine_note: The four sentences describe one specific process in sequence. B lays the snow down and presses it into ice, C traps the air inside it, D recovers those pockets by drilling, and E reads the ancient atmosphere from them. Formation, capture, retrieval, reading.

core_roles.label: B
core_roles.why_here: It begins the physical process everything else follows, the annual snow becoming glacial ice.

core_roles.label: C
core_roles.why_here: It follows directly from the compaction in B, adding the crucial step: the ice traps a sample of that year's air.

core_roles.label: D
core_roles.why_here: It moves from nature's work to the scientist's, recovering the trapped pockets in order by drilling a core.

core_roles.label: E
core_roles.why_here: It states the payoff the whole process was building toward: reading an atmosphere older than any instrument.

links.explanation: B's compaction is C's given, and C adds the new fact: as the snow compacts, it seals air inside.
D's those pockets points straight back at the trapped air C just described, moving from capture to retrieval.
D's recovered bubbles become E's given, and E adds what measuring them yields: the ancient air's composition.

explanation.violation_named: Sentence A is a general statement inside a specific narrative. The four sentences trace one concrete process, snow to ice to trapped air to reading it. A makes a broad, true claim about how science works in general, at a completely different level of abstraction from the process the paragraph is describing.
explanation.why_it_breaks: A talks about hypotheses and evidence in the abstract; the core never states a hypothesis, tests a prediction, or discusses one surviving. Every core sentence is a physical step in one method. A floats above all of them, connected to none, because it describes science as a whole rather than this particular way of reading the past.
explanation.trap_exposed: A sounds authoritative and it shares the word evidence with the spirit of the passage, so it can feel like a fitting opening line. That is the pull of a true general statement: correctness is mistaken for belonging. But the core is specific throughout, and a sentence that could open almost any science paragraph belongs to none in particular.

exclusion_analysis.label: B
exclusion_analysis.why_tempting: It is the plainest sentence, describing weather rather than science, so it can look too ordinary to be load bearing.
exclusion_analysis.what_breaks: Without B the process has no beginning: there is no ice for C to trap air in, and the account starts mid step.

exclusion_analysis.label: C
exclusion_analysis.why_tempting: Read alone it looks like a restatement of B, as though the compaction point were simply being repeated.
exclusion_analysis.what_breaks: Without C the crucial mechanism is gone, the trapping of air, and D recovers pockets the paragraph never said were there.

exclusion_analysis.label: D
exclusion_analysis.why_tempting: D and A both mention the work of science, so a hurried read may hold both as candidates for the odd one.
exclusion_analysis.what_breaks: Without D there is no retrieval, and E reads gases from bubbles that were never brought up out of the glacier.

exclusion_analysis.label: E
exclusion_analysis.why_tempting: It is the longest sentence and the only one that reaches thousands of years back, so its scale feels different.
exclusion_analysis.what_breaks: Without E the process has no purpose stated: the core is drilled and the bubbles recovered, but nothing is ever read from them.

explanation.detection_habit: When one sentence speaks in general truths and the others describe a specific process, suspect the general one. A paragraph settles at one level of abstraction, and the sentence flying above it rarely belongs.

mentor.challenge: One sentence is true of all science. The others are true of one glacier. Only one of those two kinds of sentence builds this paragraph.
mentor.takeaway: A general truth can be correct and still be in the wrong paragraph. Match the altitude, not just the topic.
